Title: Genetic compensation of triacylglycerol biosynthesis in the green microalga Chlamydomonas reinhardtii

Common_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: CC4502; CC4425; CC5165; CC5166; Δvtc1/pdat double mutant
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: PDAT knockout reduced TAG accumulation under P deprivation; PDAT upregulation compensated DGTT1 loss and contributed to elevated TAG in Δvtc1 under P deprivation.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Redox/electron sink under stress; helps maintain lipid and redox homeostasis during abiotic stress.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Knockout mutant phenotyping; double-mutant genetic analysis; qRT-PCR; Western blot; TAG quantification by TLC/GC-MS; Nile red staining
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. pdat knockout caused ~25–30% lower TAG under P deprivation, with no compensatory upregulation of DGTT1-3. 2. PDAT was strongly upregulated in dgtt1 and dgtt2 knockout mutants and compensated for TAG production. 3. In Δvtc1, PDAT expression increased 2.8-fold under P deprivation and correlated with ~90% higher TAG. 4. In Δvtc1/pdat, loss of PDAT triggered DGTT1 upregulation and even higher TAG accumulation.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Upregulated under P deprivation in dgtt1, dgtt2, dgtt3, and Δvtc1 backgrounds; non-detectable in pdat and Δvtc1/pdat knockout backgrounds.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Phosphorus deprivation; nitrogen deprivation; nutrient-replete versus nutrient-depleted conditions
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Compensation was not reciprocal: DGTT knockouts induced PDAT upregulation, but pdat knockout did not induce DGTT compensation in the wild-type background. PDAT likely mediates membrane lipid recycling for TAG synthesis under some conditions.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Important caution for algal lipid engineering: PDAT-related phenotypes can be masked by genetic compensation in DGTT null mutants; combine multiple mutant alleles/approaches and confirm with knockdown.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: pdat single mutant showed reduced TAG but no major viability or gross growth defect reported; in Δvtc1/pdat, increased TAG coincided with larger cells and increased dry weight under P deprivation.

Common_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: CC5325-derived backgrounds; CC3403-derived background; Δvtc1-related backgrounds
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Associated with TAG biosynthesis via membrane lipid recycling; transcription upregulated in dgtt2 but unchanged in dgtt1 and Δvtc1.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Redox/electron sink under stress; contributes to stress survival.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: qRT-PCR expression analysis in knockout backgrounds; literature-supported functional context discussed in Results/Discussion
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PGD1 expression was monitored as a TAG-related gene potentially functioning with PDAT in membrane recycling. 2. It was upregulated 7.6-fold in dgtt2 under P deprivation but unchanged in dgtt1 and Δvtc1. 3. The paper did not directly test PGD1 knockout/overexpression here, so its role is supportive rather than primary in this study.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Upregulated in dgtt2 under P deprivation; unchanged in dgtt1 and Δvtc1-related analyses.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included among experimentally examined TAG-biosynthesis-associated genes, but not a primary focal knockout in this paper.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential auxiliary target for engineering membrane lipid recycling into TAG, but direct causal engineering evidence in this paper is limited.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of VTC1 increased TAG accumulation under P deprivation and created a background in which PDAT became important and PDAT loss triggered DGTT1 compensation.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Redox/electron sink under stress; contributes to stress homeostasis.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant/rescue comparison; double-mutant genetic analysis; qRT-PCR; TAG quantification; Nile red staining; growth analysis
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Δvtc1 showed ~90% higher TAG than VTC1 rescue under P deprivation but not under P-replete conditions. 2. In Δvtc1, PDAT was specifically upregulated 2.8-fold, suggesting altered TAG control. 3. In Δvtc1/pdat, DGTT1 was strongly induced and TAG rose even further, indicating context-dependent compensation.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: VTC1 disruption effects manifested under P deprivation; rescue line used as control.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Phosphorus deprivation versus phosphorus replete conditions
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: VTC1 is not a TAG enzyme itself but creates the physiological context revealing PDAT dependence and DGTT1 compensation. The paper notes broader effects on P and lipid metabolism beyond direct TAG control.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful systems-level target/context for inducing TAG accumulation under phosphate stress, but pleiotropic effects on P metabolism require caution.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Potential broader perturbation of phosphate metabolism and cell physiology; larger cell dry weight in Δvtc1/pdat under stress.

Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as a rate-limiting gene for TAG biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Acylates diacylglycerol using acyl-CoA to form triacylglycerol.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: DGTT1 knockout did not reduce TAG; instead TAG increased because PDAT was upregulated. In Δvtc1/pdat, DGTT1 was highly upregulated and associated with greatly increased TAG.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Redox/electron sink under abiotic stress; supports lipid/redox homeostasis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: CLiP insertional knockout; CRISPR/Cas9 knockout; double-mutant analysis; qRT-PCR; TAG quantification by TLC/GC-MS
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Null dgtt1 mutants in two strain backgrounds accumulated equal or more TAG than controls under P deprivation. 2. DGTT1 loss triggered strong PDAT upregulation, indicating genetic compensation. 3. In the Δvtc1/pdat background, DGTT1 was upregulated 12.4-fold and likely compensated for PDAT loss, driving a 272.3% TAG increase versus VTC1.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Transcript absent in dgtt1 knockout lines; strongly upregulated in dgtt2 and Δvtc1/pdat under P deprivation.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Compensation occurred regardless of strain background or mutagenesis method. Knockout phenotype contrasted with previous knockdown phenotype, highlighting transcriptional adaptation.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Promising TAG engineering target, but null alleles can trigger compensation and confound phenotype; validation should include knockdown and multiple independent mutant alleles.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Direct knockout may not reduce TAG due to compensation; context-dependent effects on membrane lipid metabolism via compensatory PDAT upregulation.

Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control point suggested; not explicitly singled out as strongly as DGTT1
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Acylates diacylglycerol using acyl-CoA to produce triacylglycerol.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: dgtt2 knockout increased TAG accumulation under P deprivation, associated with upregulation of DGTT1, DGTT3, PDAT and PGD1.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Redox/electron sink under abiotic stress; supports stress homeostasis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: CLiP insertional knockout; qRT-PCR; TAG quantification by TLC/GC-MS
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. dgtt2 null mutant accumulated ~70% more TAG per cell and 76% more per dry weight under P deprivation. 2. Loss of DGTT2 triggered broad transcriptional compensation, including 19-fold DGTT1 and 9-fold PDAT upregulation. 3. Increased TAG in dgtt2 contrasts with previous knockdown phenotypes, supporting genetic compensation.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Transcript absent in dgtt2 knockout; upregulation seen for other TAG genes in dgtt2 background under P deprivation.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Broadest compensatory response among tested DGTT knockouts.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Engineering or functional analysis of DGTT2 requires caution because knockout can trigger compensatory network activation rather than expected TAG reduction.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Null mutation may cause unexpected TAG increase and network rewiring.

Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Possibly critical determinant for TAG biosynthesis in this species
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Acylates diacylglycerol using acyl-CoA to produce triacylglycerol.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: dgtt3 knockout reduced TAG by ~40% despite increased PDAT expression, indicating incomplete or ineffective compensation.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Redox/electron sink under abiotic stress; supports stress homeostasis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: CLiP insertional knockout; qRT-PCR; TAG quantification by TLC/GC-MS
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. dgtt3 null mutant had ~40% lower TAG than control under P deprivation. 2. PDAT was transcriptionally and translationally upregulated, but this did not restore TAG levels. 3. The authors infer DGTT3 may be a critical gene for TAG biosynthesis in Chlamydomonas.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Transcript absent in dgtt3 knockout; PDAT increased in dgtt3 background.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Shows that compensation is gene-dependent and not always sufficient to maintain product output.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential strong leverage point for TAG engineering because its loss substantially lowers TAG, but compensatory responses and possible post-transcriptional regulation should be considered.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: TAG reduction under stress may impair stress tolerance or redox balance, although this was not directly quantified here.
